questions.questionText: The most appropriate initial fluid for septic shock resuscitation is:
questions.explanation: Crystalloids are first-line for initial fluid resuscitation in septic shock.

questions.questionText: Mean arterial pressure target generally recommended early in septic shock is around:
questions.explanation: A target MAP of about 65 mmHg is commonly used in early septic shock management.

questions.questionText: The first-line vasopressor in septic shock is:
questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is the preferred first-line vasopressor in septic shock.

questions.questionText: In ARDS, a lung-protective ventilation strategy includes:
questions.optionA: Tidal volume 10-12 mL per kg predicted body weight
questions.optionB: Tidal volume around 6 mL per kg predicted body weight
questions.optionD: Routine hyperventilation to very low PaCO2
questions.explanation: Low tidal volume ventilation reduces ventilator-induced lung injury in ARDS.

questions.questionText: A key criterion for diagnosis of ARDS includes:
questions.optionB: Acute hypoxemia with bilateral opacities not fully explained by cardiac failure
questions.explanation: ARDS is defined by acute onset hypoxemia and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates of non-cardiac origin.

questions.questionText: The most important intervention in anaphylactic shock is:
questions.explanation: Prompt intramuscular epinephrine is life-saving and first-line in anaphylaxis.

questions.questionText: In diabetic ketoacidosis, initial management includes all except:
questions.explanation: Bicarbonate is not routinely indicated in DKA and is reserved for severe acidosis in select cases.

questions.questionText: The preferred immediate intervention for unstable wide-complex tachycardia with hypotension is:
questions.explanation: Hemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias require immediate synchronized cardioversion.

questions.questionText: For hyperkalemia with ECG changes, the first stabilizing drug is:
questions.explanation: IV calcium stabilizes cardiac membrane and is first for life-threatening hyperkalemic ECG changes.

questions.questionText: The best bedside score commonly used to estimate mortality in ICU patients is:
questions.explanation: APACHE II is widely used for severity and mortality estimation in critical care.

questions.questionText: In status epilepticus not responding to initial benzodiazepine, a common next-line antiepileptic is:
questions.explanation: Second-line therapy after benzodiazepines includes levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate.

questions.questionText: For prevention of stress-related mucosal bleeding in high-risk ventilated ICU patients, commonly used prophylaxis includes:
questions.explanation: Stress ulcer prophylaxis with PPI or H2 blocker is used in selected high-risk ICU patients.

questions.questionText: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle includes all EXCEPT:
questions.optionC: Administer steroids to all patients immediately
questions.optionD: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics and begin rapid fluid resuscitation
questions.explanation: Hour-1 bundle: (1) Measure lactate (re-measure if >2), (2) Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, (3) Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, (4) Begin rapid 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4, (5) Apply vasopressors if hypotensive during/after fluid resuscitation (target MAP ≥65). Steroids are NOT routine — reserved for refractory shock (stress-dose hydrocortisone). Procalcitonin: may guide antibiotic duration. qSOFA: rapid bedside screen (≥2 of: RR≥22, altered mentation, SBP≤100). Reference: Harrison — Surviving Sepsis.

questions.questionText: The most reliable predictor for successful weaning from mechanical ventilation is:
questions.optionB: Rapid shallow breathing index (RSBI = f/VT) <105 breaths/min/L during spontaneous breathing trial
questions.explanation: RSBI (Rapid Shallow Breathing Index): respiratory rate (f) divided by tidal volume (VT in liters) during a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT). RSBI <105: predicts successful extubation (high sensitivity). SBT: 30-120 min on T-piece or low PS. Criteria for weaning readiness: improving underlying condition, adequate oxygenation (FiO2 ≤40%, PEEP ≤5-8), hemodynamic stability, ability to breathe spontaneously, mental alertness. Cuff leak test: assesses for laryngeal edema. Reference: Harrison — Ventilator Weaning.

questions.questionText: Brain death is confirmed by demonstrating:
questions.optionB: Absent brainstem reflexes (pupillary, corneal, oculocephalic, gag, cough) AND positive apnea test (no respiratory effort with PaCO2 >60 mmHg)
questions.explanation: Brain death: irreversible cessation of all brain function including brainstem. Clinical criteria: (1) Known irreversible cause, (2) Exclude confounders (hypothermia <32°C, drugs, metabolic derangements), (3) Absent brainstem reflexes: fixed dilated pupils, absent corneal reflex, absent oculocephalic/oculovestibular (caloric), absent gag/cough, (4) Positive apnea test: no respiratory effort when PaCO2 rises >60 mmHg or >20 above baseline. Confirmatory tests (if clinical exam limited): cerebral angiography, nuclear scan, EEG (supportive, not sufficient alone). Reference: Harrison — Brain Death.
